title: Marking and clipping a queen bee
subtitle: How to spot, catch, mark, and optionally clip your queen before returning her to the colony.
excerpt: A marked queen is a known queen. One small coloured dot on her thorax tells you at a glance which year she was introduced, whether this is still the queen you started with, and whether she survived the winter. The clipping step is optional but worth understanding.
sourceNotes: National Bee Unit, Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024), chapter on queen management. BBKA Technical Bulletin on queen marking and clipping. Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Internet Archive, chapter 6 on finding and handling queens.

glossaryTerms.definition: The middle section of a bee's body, between the head and the abdomen. The point where the wings and legs attach. The correct surface for queen marking.

Removing part of one or both of the queen's wings with small scissors to prevent her leading a swarm flight. A swarm with a clipped queen cannot fly far and usually returns to the hive or clusters near the entrance.

The colony's process of raising a replacement queen while the old queen is still present. Different from an emergency queen cell — supersedure is planned by the bees, not triggered by sudden queenlessness.

content.text: A marked queen is easier to find on every subsequent inspection. One coloured dot on her
thorax
confirms she is alive, identifies her year of introduction, and distinguishes her from any
supersedure
cell that produced a replacement. Do this in spring when the colony is growing and the queen is easy to find.
The international colour code

The BIBBA and IBRA colour system assigns one colour per year ending: white (1 or 6), yellow (2 or 7), red (3 or 8), green (4 or 9), blue (5 or 0). A queen introduced in 2024 gets a green dot; one introduced in 2025 gets a blue dot. Knowing the colour tells you whether the colony has quietly requeened itself over winter.
Before you open the hive

Light the smoker and suit up fully. This is a procedure that demands patience; a rushed inspection leads to a rolled queen. Choose a warm still day above 15°C with foragers on the wing; the colony will be calmer and the queen easier to spot.
Finding the queen

Remove the crown board and apply two or three puffs of smoke under it before lifting. Work from the side of the box away from the entrance. Lift the first brood frame out and hold it at eye level, tilting slightly to catch the sun across the comb surface. The queen is longer than the workers, with a smooth pointed abdomen and shorter wings relative to her body. She tends to move steadily across the comb rather than circling; if you lose sight of her, put the frame back exactly as you lifted it and check the adjacent one.

attrs.title: What a queen looks like
attrs.body: Longer abdomen than workers, often slightly more golden. Wings cover only about two-thirds of the abdomen. Workers tend to turn to face her as she passes; watch for that ripple effect on a busy brood frame. In a spring colony with few bees she is easy to spot; in a summer colony of 60,000 she can take ten minutes of careful searching.

content.text: Catching and marking

Place the queen-marking cage over her gently, pressing it flat against the comb so she walks into it. Do not tip or shake the frame while she is under the cage. Once she is inside, press the plunger down until she is held lightly against the mesh, firm enough to keep her still, not so firm as to damage her abdomen. Uncap the marker pen on a piece of tissue first to check the flow, then apply one small dot to the centre of the
thorax
. Avoid the wing bases and the head. Wait five seconds for the paint to dry before releasing.
Clipping: the decision and the method
The
queen clipping

procedure removes roughly half of one forewing with small scissors. It does not prevent a swarm from building; it prevents the queen from leaving with it. If a swarm departs with a clipped queen, she falls near the entrance and the swarm returns to the hive or clusters just outside. This gives you 24 hours to deal with the swarm cell before the first virgin queen emerges.

To clip: with the queen still held in the marking cage, use small sharp scissors to remove the front half of one forewing. Keep the cut clean and quick. Release her onto the brood frame immediately afterwards, not into the air. Some beekeepers clip as a matter of course; others dislike it on welfare grounds. Both positions are reasonable. If you are not sure, mark without clipping.
Returning the queen

Lower the marked queen gently onto the brood frame and watch until she moves off the frame edge and into the cluster. Do not close the hive until you have seen her accepted. If workers are balling her (a tight moving cluster around one point), pause and give another light puff of smoke before gently brushing the cluster with the bee brush. A freshly marked queen that smells of paint will occasionally be balled by a defensive colony; this is temporary in most cases and resolves within a few minutes.

entries.cue: Cannot find the queen after checking all frames twice
entries.cause: She may be on the bottom of the brood box, on the hive wall, or already on a frame you replaced
entries.fix: Lay all frames against the outside of the hive wall, facing the comb outwards. Check the box floor and walls. Go through frames again one by one starting from the most brood-dense centre frames.

entries.cue: Workers balling the queen after marking
entries.cause: Paint smell is irritating to the colony; can also happen after any handling if the queen's pheromone signal is disturbed
entries.fix: Apply light smoke around the ball. The balling usually disperses in 3 to 5 minutes. Do not try to pull the ball apart manually.

entries.cue: Marker ink does not dry before the queen moves
entries.cause: Paint pen nib is oversaturated; dot was too large
entries.fix: Practise the dot size on a piece of card before the inspection. One small touch of the pen is enough; a large wet dot takes longer to dry and can drip onto the wing bases.
